NY.organization: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
NY.project_title: Conditional M. tuberculosis knockdown mutants in mice with human-like pathology
NY.public_health_relevance:
Tuberculosis (TB) is still a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide and a better understanding of its
pathogenesis will aid the development of strategies to prevent and treat this disease. TB mouse models have
provided important insights into host responses and immune mechanisms of M. tuberculosis (Mtb) control, but
most studies but have not been performed in mouse strains that recapitulate the diverse and heterogeneous
environments Mtb encounters in humans. Here we propose to develop TB latency models with conditional Mtb
knockdown mutants and the C3HeB/FeJ mouse strain that develops human-like pathology.

NY.project_title: Probing the novel mechanism of action of tuberculocidal compounds
NY.public_health_relevance:
NARRATIVE
Due to increasing rates of drug resistance, new antibiotics are urgently needed to treat
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, the deadliest infectious disease in human history. We have
identified a small molecule that inhibits Mycobacterium tuberculosis through a novel
molecular mechanism, and therefore holds promising potential in mitigating common
drug resistance mechanisms. Here we present a detailed plan to characterize this
molecule and validate its molecular target, providing a new potential therapeutic
strategy to treat this tenacious pathogen.

NY.project_title: Role of Wnt/β-catenin pathway in alveolar epithelial repair during tuberculosis and its regulation by chronic type I interferon signaling
NY.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
Tuberculosis (TB), caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), remains a major global health
challenge, worsened by disruptions in care during the COVID-19 pandemic. This study investigates
how Mtb-induced type I interferons (IFN-I) contribute to lung barrier injury and impair alveolar
epithelial repair, with a focus on the regulatory crosstalk between IFN-I and Wnt/β-catenin signaling.
By identifying therapeutic targets to restore lung homeostasis, this work aims to inform new
interventions that reduce TB-associated lung damage and improve patient outcomes.

NY.organization: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
NY.project_title: Salicylate-based Drugs as Adjuvants to Potentiate Bedaquiline's Anti-TB Activity
NY.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
Bedaquiline is the first new anti-tuberculosis (anti-TB) drug approved in over 40 years and
currently serves as the backbone of nearly all second line regimens for drug resistant TB. This
proposal aims to study the utility of salicylate-based non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as
adjunctive therapies to improve the anti-TB activity of bedaquiline. Enhanced bedaquiline
activity can ultimately lead to shorter treatment regimens for drug resistant TB and help
overcome existing low-level resistance to bedaquiline, thereby preserving the clinical utility of
bedaquiline; as well as other anti-TB drugs used in conjunction with bedaquiline.

NY.project_title: Optimization of Antitubercular Compounds Against a Novel Mycobacterium tuberculosis target, AspC
NY.public_health_relevance:
NARRATIVE
Due to increasing rates of drug resistance, new antibiotics are urgently needed to treat
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, the deadliest infectious disease in human history. We have
identified a potent small molecule with favorable drug-like characteristics that inhibits
Mycobacterium tuberculosis through a novel molecular mechanism, and therefore holds
promising potential in mitigating common drug resistance mechanisms. Here we present a
detailed plan to optimize this molecule and validate its molecular target, providing a new
potential therapeutic strategy to treat this tenacious pathogen.

NY.project_title: Chalkophore mediated respiratory oxidase flexibility and M. tuberculosis virulence
NY.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chalkophore mediated respiratory oxidase flexibility and M. tuberculosis virulence
Diisonitrile chalkophores are a new class of small-molecule natural products produced by bacteria that bind
tightly to copper and can be used to transport this micronutrient into the bacterial cell. This project will use a
combined chemical and biological approach to investigate the roles of chalkophores in bacterial
cell physiology and pathogenesis with a particular focus on functions in Mycobacterium tuberculosis,
the bacteria that cause tuberculosis.

MA.project_title: Accelerating discovery of narrow-spectrum antibiotics for Lyme disease
MA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Identification of new narrow-spectrum antibiotics could have a major impact through multiple uses in prevention
and treatment of infectious disease. We propose to develop new computational and experimental technologies
that can dramatically accelerate the narrow-spectrum drug development pipeline for Lyme disease, a major
health concern in endemic areas of the U.S. When this project is completed, we hope to contribute expandable
validated tools to rapidly screen for active compounds and identify mechanisms of drug action and novel narrow-
spectrum antibiotic candidates for Lyme disease.

MA.project_title: Regulation of innate immunity by the pathogen Staphylococcus aureus
MA.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
Staphylococcus aureus is a leading cause of bacteria-induced morbidity and mortality in the United States. Our
lack of knowledge of how S. aureus interfaces with the immune system undermines vaccine development in this
important area of biology. Our research proposal focuses on the earliest triggers of immune activation (the
detection of bacteria), which may unravel new means to control the activation or inactivation of immunity.

MA.project_title: Spatial multi-omics to predict granuloma trajectory and bacterial restriction during vaccination
MA.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
Vaccine development for TB has focused on attempting to identify correlates of vaccine protection. Recent
success using IV vaccination in the rhesus macaque model of Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) suggests that
development of tissue resident memory cells in the lung is a key correlate of protection; however, there is an
incomplete understanding of where these cells are located and how they influence the development of
granulomas in response to different vaccine modalities. Here, we propose to develop a pipeline of spatial
phenotyping of lung responses during vaccination and to develop multiparameter models to correlate tissue
phenotypes with peripheral immune responses.

MA.project_title: Mechanisms of the alternative macrophage activation in TB
MA.public_health_relevance:
Narrative: Mechanisms of the alternative macrophage activation in TB
Pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) remains a leading cause of death from a single infectious agent, Mycobacterium
tuberculosis (Mtb). Lung vulnerability to TB represents a major obstacle for the development of efficacious
vaccines and therapies, but mechanisms of the particular lung vulnerability to TB remain unknown. We will
characterize the alternatively activated macrophages that uniquely appear during progression of pulmonary TB
in genetically susceptible hosts using advanced single cell analyses of the blood and multiplexed
immunohistochemistry of TB lesions, and investigate the effects of known drivers of the alternative macrophage
activation on their induction during pulmonary TB progression.

MA.organization: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
MA.project_title: Eliciting Th1/Th17 dual responses for vaccine-induced immunity to tuberculosis
MA.public_health_relevance:
Project narrative. Bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) is the primary vaccine used against
Mycobacterium tuberculosis. This research proposal seeks to identify immune
signatures that can be used to identify a successful vaccine against Mtb, and the
mechanisms that will lead to a vaccine that can elicit this response.

MA.project_title: A tissue-based approach to understanding TB-associated lung fibrosis
MA.public_health_relevance:
Tuberculosis remains a leading cause of death around the world, and new treatments
are needed to curb the epidemic. This work will help us understand how fibrosis and
permanent lung damage happen in TB infection. We anticipate that this work will
facilitate future development of new anti-tuberculosis treatments that improve quality and
longevity after TB infection.

MA.project_title: Investigation of factors involved in assembly of the mycolata envelope
MA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT SUMMARY
Bacteria within the Corynebacterineae suborder, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Corynebacterium
diphtheriae, pose significant global healthcare challenges, partly due to the distinct cell envelope of these
bacteria, which plays a crucial role in their pathogenicity and antibiotic resistance. My research focuses on
understanding how the complex mycobacterial cell envelope layers are synthesized to identify new weak points
to exploit for antibiotic development.

CA.project_title: Deorphanizing a novel GPCR expressed by tissue resident memory cells
CA.public_health_relevance:
RELEVANCE
Lung infections cause substantial mortality and morbidity in children and adults across all ages. A wide-range of
viruses, bacteria and fungi can infect the lungs of healthy and immuno-compromised patients. Current strategies
to prevent or treat these infections have limitations that are exacerbated by the growing risk of multi-drug
resistance and emergence of new pathogens. Understanding the immunological basis of protective immune
responses to respiratory infections will help develop approaches to improve vaccine and treatment efficacy.
Tissue-resident memory T cells (TRM cells) have emerged as the guardians of mucosal and lung immunity. Thus
approaches to boost TRM response are likely to offer durable natural and vaccine-induced immunity. In this
proposal, we are finding potential targets that can to boost lung immune (TRM) responses.

CA.project_title: Immunogenetic determinants of response to drug-susceptible tuberculosis treatment
CA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Responses to tuberculosis treatment vary, with many persons exhibiting signs of treatment failure (incomplete
cure) by the end of standard therapy or developing recurrent disease after finishing treatment. It remains
unclear who would benefit from shorter treatments to avoid side effects of toxic antibiotics, and who would
need standard or intensified regimens. We will leverage data from three tuberculosis treatment clinical trials
testing standard of care, shortened, and novel regimens to identify factors associated with favorable and
unfavorable outcomes, to test whether we can stratify persons with tuberculosis to different regimens and
durations of treatment.

CA.project_title: Characterization of a novel histidine phosphotransfer system involved in the virulence of Mycobacterium tuberculosis
CA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative agent of tuberculosis (TB), is still one of the deadliest diseases
afflicting the world’s population, therefore there is urgent need to develop novel anti-TB therapeutics. Metabolic
pathways are good therapeutic drug targets, and this proposal focuses on a metabolic protein of unknown
function that is linked to Mtb virulence. Thus, this proposal may result in new therapeutic targets for drug
development, which has the potential to have an extraordinary impact on global health.

CA.project_title: Exploring leaderless mRNA translation, a potential Achilles heel of mycobacteria
CA.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
Tuberculosis (TB) remains a significant global health threat, causing approximately 1.5 million deaths annually,
and the increasing number of drug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) strains makes reducing this
burden even more difficult. This project seeks to increase our understanding of a prevalent but poorly
understood form of translation in mycobacteria, the translation of leaderless mRNAs. Knowledge of the
mechanisms that allow lmRNA translation to occur and a deeper understanding of its contribution to the
mycobacterial life cycle will contribute to the development of novel therapeutics exploiting this form of
translation, increasing treatment options for drug resistant Mtb and enhancing public health.

MO.project_title: Molecular mechanisms of MCP-1 polymorphism-mediated susceptibility to pulmonary tuberculosis infection
MO.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
 Host genetic variations play an important role in tuberculosis (TB) susceptibility and latent TB infection (LTBI)
reactivation. One single nucleotide polymorphism G/G (SNP-G) at -2518 in the promoter region of monocyte
chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1) is strongly associated with active pulmonary TB. This project will help us
understand the molecular mechanisms of how the -2518 G/G homozygote in the MCP-1 promoter contributes to
TB susceptibility and reveal a novel role for a group of transcription factors called E-box binding proteins in
control of host defense against Mtb infection.

MI.organization: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
MI.project_title: Genetic control of early Mycobacterium tuberculosis inflammatory responses
MI.public_health_relevance:
Narrative:
Infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis can lead to inflammatory lung disease and long-term deficits in
pulmonary function in some individuals. We will investigate the role of genetic variation in the key interactions
between this bacterium and alveolar macrophages in the lung. Through this work, we will test the hypothesis
that alveolar macrophage responses are an important determinant of infection outcome, and generate a
broadly useful experimental platform for studying this enigmatic cell type.

OR.project_title: Identifying drug targets in M. abscessus through the application of fluorescent probes
OR.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
The proposed project fits the NIAID's goal to “…better understand Pulmonary Non-Tuberculous Mycobacteria
(NTM) Infections and inform development of medical countermeasures.” This project will use novel chemical
probes to determine the underlying mechanism of action for dual β-lactam antibiotics against the highly drug-
resistant NTM Mycobacterium abscessus.

NC.public_health_relevance:
Relevance Statement
Bladder infections are a significant medical problem because of its high rate of recurrence, an
attribute related to bacterial persistence in the bladder long after infection has resolved. We
have recently discovered that intravesical administration of a vaccine formulation that includes
the live attenuated vaccine, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG), is highly effective in eliminating
bacteria harboring in the bladder. Here, we propose to demonstrate the efficacy and durability
of this novel immunotherapy when applied alone and in combination with vaccine antigens and
to explore its underling basis of action.

NC.organization: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
NC.project_title: Leadership and Operations Center (LOC), AIDS Clinical Trials Group
NC.public_health_relevance:
Project Narrative
The studies proposed in this application will have a direct beneficial effect on the health of millions of people
worldwide who are infected with or at risk for HIV, tuberculosis and hepatitis B, transforming the health of
people with these infections. The clinical research conducted by the ACTG will lead to significantly reducing
morbidity and mortality, particularly among populations disproportionately affected by HIV/AIDS.

GA.project_title: Establishing a cystic fibrosis ferret model of chronic Mycobacterium abscessus lung infection
GA.public_health_relevance:
Project narrative
 The proposed research is relevant to public health because it proposes to establish a
novel, physiologically relevant and urgently needed animal model to study Mycobacterium
abscessus lung infection in cystic fibrosis that represents a significant medical problem. No animal
models exist that appropriately model chronic M. abs. lung infection in cystic fibrosis. The
proposed research is, therefore, important to the mission of the NIH by establishing a new animal
model suitable to study disease pathogenesis of chronic M. abs. lung infection in cystic fibrosis
and to test new therapeutics.

GA.project_title: Cavity and Granuloma Oriented Inflammation and Tissue Pharmacokinetics in Pulmonary Tuberculosis (COOK TB)
GA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pulmonary tuberculosis including drug-resistant disease is an enormous public health problem responsible for
substantial morbidity and mortality worldwide with the number of deaths increasing for the first time in over two
decades. This proposal seeks to better understand the host inflammatory response to Mycobacterium
tuberculosis in individual human lung granulomas and to map the spatial distribution of newly implemented
drugs used to treat drug-resistant disease within granuloma microenvironments. The long-term goals of this
work are to provide data that will help guide the 1) development of host directed therapy to limit lung tissue
damage and 2) design of optimal drug combinations to improve treatment and long-term outcomes among
patients with pulmonary tuberculosis.

MD.project_title: Characterizing NLRP11 Activation by Mycobacteria to Enhance Innate Immune Responses
MD.public_health_relevance:
We will characterize how the non-canonical NLRP11/caspase-4 inflammasome recognizes
mycobacteria.

CT.project_title: Translational studies of hookworm infection in Ghana
CT.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hookworm infection is a leading cause of anemia and malnutrition in poor countries, especially in sub-Saharan
Africa. Using both field-based and advanced genomic techniques, we will conduct studies aimed at defining
host and parasite factors that mediate hookworm infection, response to deworming and reinfection in endemic
settings. The studies proposed here will provide novel insights that will aid future development of effective
control strategies for human hookworm infection.

LA.organization: LOUISIANA STATE UNIV A&M COL BATON ROUGE
LA.project_title: Role of host glutaminolysis in tuberculosis pathogenesis

PA.project_title: Estimation of risk associated with zoonotic tuberculosis in South India
PA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tuberculosis (TB) is a major public health threat that spreads readily among humans or from animals to humans
(termed zoonotic tuberculosis or zTB). Yet we lack accurate estimates of zTB risk, especially in high-TB-burden
settings where the disease is endemic in both humans and livestock and where there are greater opportunities
for transmission of zTB from animals to humans. Our innovative, rigorously designed, and first-of-its-kind study
will fill this major knowledge gap by assessing the risks associated with zTB, and thereby enable the development
of evidence-based approaches to reduce spillover from livestock to humans.

PA.project_title: Estimation of risk associated with zoonotic tuberculosis in South India
PA.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE

null.organization: ASOCIACION CIVIL IMPACTA SALUD Y EDUCACN
null.public_health_relevance: The PERU CTU proposes to continue contributing to the design and implementation of the DAIDS scientific research agenda to decrease new HIV infections, reduce HIV associated morbidity and mortality, and ultimately end the HIV epidemic. Given the high incidence of tuberculosis in the country and the high burden of drug resistant strains, the PERU CTU is in an advantageous position to continue contributing to the design and implementation of clinical trials evaluating interventions to control the tuberculosis epidemic.

null.project_title: Thailand HIV/AIDS and Infectious Disease Clinical Trials Unit (THAI CTU)
null.public_health_relevance:
Thailand HIV/AIDS and Infectious Disease Clinical Trials Unit (THAI CTU)
 SF 424 (R&R) Other Project Information – Project Narrative
The Thailand HIV/AIDS and Infectious Disease Clinical Trials Unit (THAI CTU) is a collaboration between the
Chiang Mai University-Research Institute for Health Sciences (CMU-RIHES) in Chiang Mai, Thailand, and the
Thai Red Cross AIDS Research Centre (TRC-ARC) in Bangkok, Thailand. The renewed THAI CTU and its four
CRSs will participate in developing and conducting clinical trials proposed by the four NIH HIV/AIDS Clinical
Trials Networks: HIV/AIDS adult therapeutics; HIV/AIDS maternal, adolescent and pediatric therapeutics; HIV
prevention; and HIV vaccines. The collaboration between the NIH HIV/AIDS Clinical Trials Networks and their
affiliated CTUs, including the THAI CTU, will lead to development of innovative and effective measures for HIV
treatment and prevention, and ultimately to HIV cure and an end to the global HIV epidemic.

WA.project_title: HIV Vaccines Clinical Trials Network Leadership and Operations Center
WA.public_health_relevance:
Despite the increasing availability and effectiveness of antiretroviral therapy, HIV continues to be the greatest
pandemic of the last 50 years — 70 million infected, 36 million currently living with HIV and over 1.8 million
new infections, including over 180,000 new infant infections annually — so it is imperative to develop an
effective population-based approach to reduce transmission. This proposal for the Leadership and Operations
Center of the HIV Vaccine Trials Network, the collaboration of scientists at 64 clinical trial sites in 15 countries
on 4 continents dedicated to developing a globally effective HIV vaccine, describes the marked advances in
candidate vaccines to induce broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs), the advances in bnAbs administered by
injection at levels that can prevent infection, and the HVTN’s extensive programs in engaging both local
communities and other scientists throughout the world working to stop the spread of HIV. The goal of the
HVTN is to develop a vaccine or monoclonal antibody combination that will, either alone or in combination,
reduce HIV acquisition in adults and children by more than 60 percent.

SC.project_title: Metabolic Syndrome Outcomes Among Patients with Tuberculosis in Tanzania
SC.public_health_relevance:
PROJECT NARRATIVE
The prevalence of noncommunicable diseases such as diabetes and metabolic syndrome is increasing in
settings where infectious diseases like tuberculosis are common. The proposed clinical study will characterize
metabolic profiles of people with newly diagnosed tuberculosis, determine the dynamics of those metabolic
profiles during tuberculosis treatment, and identify risk factors for persistence of metabolic disorders, towards a
goal of eventually developing better approaches to dual-disease management. An integrated set of career
development activities will prepare Dr. Yosra Alkabab to lead future work as an independent physician-scientist
and contribute to improving human health through interventions that address the intersection of communicable
and non-communicable diseases.
